exercises.title: Write a Filtered SELECT
exercises.summary: Practice writing basic SELECT queries with WHERE and ORDER BY.
instructions: Using a sample database (e.g., the Northwind or Sakila database), write a query that returns all customers from a specific country, sorted by name.
Modify the query to return only customers who placed orders in the last 90 days.
Ensure you select specific columns, not SELECT *.

exercises.title: Debug a Broken Query
exercises.summary: Practice reading SQL errors and fixing common syntax mistakes.
instructions: Given a query with 3 deliberate errors (missing comma, misspelled keyword, wrong table name), identify and fix each error.
Run the corrected query and verify it returns the expected results.

exercises.title: Multi-Table Analytical Query
exercises.summary: Write a query joining three or more tables with aggregation.
instructions: Write a query that shows total revenue by product category for the last quarter.
Join orders, order details, products, and categories tables.
Use a CTE to compute the revenue, then query the CTE for the final result.
successCriteria: All joins are correct — no accidental row multiplication.
CTE improves readability over a nested subquery.

exercises.title: Window Function Ranking
exercises.summary: Practice ROW_NUMBER and RANK for analytical queries.
instructions: Write a query that ranks customers by total spending within each region.
Show only the top 3 customers per region.
Decide whether to use ROW_NUMBER or RANK and explain your choice.
successCriteria: Window function is correctly partitioned by region.
Choice between ROW_NUMBER and RANK is justified.

exercises.title: Design a Schema from Requirements
exercises.summary: Design a normalized schema for a booking system.
instructions: Given requirements for a room booking system (users, rooms, bookings, cancellations), design a normalized schema.
Write the DDL including all constraints (PK, FK, NOT NULL, CHECK, UNIQUE).
Write both up and down migration scripts.
successCriteria: Schema is in 3NF or deviations are documented.

exercises.title: Read an Execution Plan
exercises.summary: Analyze an execution plan and identify optimization opportunities.
instructions: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on a slow query.
Identify which operations consume the most time.
Propose at least one index that would improve performance.
Verify your proposal with a new EXPLAIN.
successCriteria: Correctly identifies the most expensive operation.
Proposed index is appropriate for the query pattern.

exercises.title: Design an Index Strategy
exercises.summary: Design a complete index strategy for a given query workload.
instructions: Given 5 common queries against a schema, design an index strategy that covers all of them.
Consider composite index column ordering.
Document the trade-off between read performance and write overhead.
Remove any redundant indexes.
successCriteria: All 5 queries benefit from the index strategy.

exercises.title: Concurrency Experiment
exercises.summary: Demonstrate isolation level behavior with concurrent transactions.
instructions: Set up two concurrent database sessions.
Demonstrate a dirty read, non-repeatable read, and phantom read scenario.
Show which isolation level prevents each anomaly.
Document the performance trade-off of stricter isolation.

exercises.title: Multi-Service Data Architecture
exercises.summary: Design data architecture for a microservices system.
instructions: Given a system with 4 services sharing customer and transaction data, design the data architecture.
Define data ownership boundaries and contracts between services.
Address consistency strategy (strong vs eventual).
Plan the migration path from a monolithic database.
successCriteria: Consistency strategy is documented with trade-offs.
Migration plan includes intermediate states and rollback.

exercises.title: Establish Data Governance
exercises.summary: Create a data governance framework for a development team.
instructions: Write a naming convention document for tables, columns, indexes, and constraints.
Design a schema change review process with approval gates.
Create a template for documenting schema decisions.
Define metrics for measuring adherence.
successCriteria: Naming conventions cover all object types.
Review process has clear roles and gates.
Documentation template is practical and complete.
